title: Quiz: BQP - Bounded-Error Quantum Polynomial Time
description: Test your understanding of the BQP complexity class and its relationships to other classes

questions.question: What is the acceptance criterion for a language L to be in BQP?

options.text: Accept with probability exactly 1 for yes-instances
Accept with probability >= 2/3 for yes-instances, <= 1/3 for no-instances

questions.explanation: BQP requires bounded error: yes-instances must be accepted with probability at least 2/3, and no-instances with probability at most 1/3. The gap between these values (1/3) is crucial for error amplification.

questions.question: Which containment is KNOWN to be true?
questions.explanation: BQP ⊆ PSPACE is proven: quantum circuit amplitudes can be computed using polynomial space via path summation. The relationships between BQP and NP remain unknown (neither containment is proven).

questions.question: How many repetitions are needed to reduce BQP error probability from 1/3 to exponentially small (1/2^n)?
questions.explanation: By the Chernoff bound, O(n) repetitions with majority voting reduces error to 2^{-Omega(n)}. The error decreases exponentially with the number of trials, so polynomial trials give exponentially small error.

questions.question: The specific choice of 2/3 and 1/3 in the BQP definition is essential - using 0.51 and 0.49 would give a different complexity class.
questions.explanation: False. Any constants bounded away from 1/2 give the same class BQP. This is because probability amplification allows converting any gap > 0 to the standard 2/3 vs 1/3 gap with only polynomial overhead.

questions.question: Which problem is believed to be in BQP but NOT in BPP?

options.text: Primality testing (determining if n is prime)
Sorting a list of n numbers

questions.explanation: Integer factoring is in BQP (via Shor's algorithm) but is believed to not be in BPP (no known efficient classical algorithm). Primality testing is actually in P (AKS algorithm), and sorting/matrix multiplication are also in P.

questions.question: What does the oracle separation between BQP and PH (polynomial hierarchy) tell us?

options.text: BQP and PH are incomparable in the real world
There is strong evidence BQP has power beyond the polynomial hierarchy
Quantum computers can solve all problems in PH efficiently

questions.explanation: The Raz-Tal oracle separation shows that relative to certain oracles, BQP is not contained in PH. While oracle results don't directly imply real-world separations, they provide strong evidence that BQP has unique computational power.

questions.question: Why can quantum circuits be simulated in PSPACE?

options.text: Quantum states can be stored in polynomial space
Individual amplitudes can be computed in polynomial space via path summation
Quantum circuits are reversible and reversibility implies space efficiency
PSPACE includes all physically realizable computations

questions.explanation: The key insight is that each individual amplitude <y|C|x> can be computed by summing over all computational paths, where each path uses only polynomial space. The sum has exponentially many terms but can be enumerated in polynomial space.

questions.question: Which of the following are BQP-complete problems? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Approximating the Jones polynomial at certain roots of unity

questions.explanation: Quantum circuit evaluation and Jones polynomial approximation are BQP-complete. Integer factoring is in BQP but not known to be BQP-complete (no proof that all BQP problems reduce to it). SAT is NP-complete, not known to be in BQP.
